metadata.summary: Translate plain-language feature requests into traceable SMART requirements, user stories, and Given-When-Then acceptance criteria for spec.md authoring.

content.title: Specification Requirements
content.summary: A structured methodology for translating a plain-language feature request into a traceable set of user personas, INVEST-validated user stories, SMART functional and non-functional requirements (FR-X / NFR-X), and Given-When-Then acceptance criteria (AC-X). Every FR must trace to a user story; every Must FR must have at least one AC. Output is a spec.md that the design phase consumes.

why.p: Ambiguous requirements are the single largest source of rework in software projects. SMART criteria make each requirement testable (pass/fail); traceability (FR-X → US-X → AC-X) ensures no requirement is implemented without a verification condition. MoSCoW prioritization enforces scope discipline — without it, every requirement becomes "Must" and MVP never ships.

list.item: Starting any non-trivial feature estimated to touch more than one system layer
Stakeholder has described a need in plain language that needs translation into testable requirements
Feature scope is ambiguous and "Out of Scope" must be made explicit before design begins
Multiple user personas are affected and their needs conflict or overlap
Acceptance criteria will drive automated BDD tests (Gherkin/Given-When-Then)
Bug fix with a clear, agreed-upon fix — spec overhead exceeds value
Purely internal refactor with no user-facing behavior change
Spike or proof-of-concept — spec assumes known requirements; spikes discover them
Design is already finalized and requirements are back-filled without intent to use them

rule.statement: Every FR must satisfy all five SMART criteria: Specific (one interpretation only), Measurable (testable pass/fail), Achievable (technically feasible), Relevant (traces to a user story), Time-bound (MoSCoW priority assigned).
rule.rationale: Ambiguous requirements are the largest source of rework. SMART forces precision at authoring time, not during implementation.

rule.statement: Use "System SHALL" imperative voice for every FR. Never use "should", "can", "may", or "will".
rule.rationale: "SHALL" is unambiguous in requirements engineering and signals a testable mandatory constraint.

rule.statement: Ban ambiguous words: "fast", "good", "easy", "support", "handle". Replace with quantified targets.
rule.rationale: These words cannot be tested — they produce requirements that pass review but fail verification.

example.title: Well-formed FR with SMART criteria
example.note: FR-001: System SHALL validate email format on registration. Validation SHALL reject any input that does not match RFC 5321. Priority: Must. Traces to: US-001.

example.title: Ambiguous FR without SMART criteria
example.note: FR-001: System should handle email validation properly. — not measurable, no trace, no priority.

rule.statement: Assign each FR exactly one MoSCoW priority: Must (cannot launch without), Should (important but not critical), Could (nice to have), Won't (explicitly excluded from this release).
rule.rationale: Without explicit prioritization, every requirement defaults to Must and MVP scope explodes.

rule.statement: Must requirements must not exceed 60% of total FR count. If they do, re-classify or split the feature.
rule.rationale: Enforcing this ratio preserves the MVP concept and prevents scope creep disguised as priority.

rule.statement: Every FR must reference at least one user story (US-X). Every Must FR must reference at least one acceptance criterion (AC-X).
rule.rationale: Traceability ensures every implemented requirement has a business reason and a verification condition.

rule.statement: Use sequential identifiers: FR-001, FR-002; US-001; AC-001. Never use section headings as identifiers.
rule.rationale: IDs are stable references across spec, design, and implementation documents. Headings change; IDs should not.

rule.statement: Write every AC in Given-When-Then format with specific values, not generic placeholders. "Given user is on registration page" is acceptable; "Given user enters valid data" is not.
rule.rationale: Specific values make ACs directly executable as BDD tests without interpretation.

rule.statement: Cover at minimum: happy path, error handling (validation failure), boundary condition, security scenario (unauthorized access or injection). Performance scenario required for any Must NFR.
rule.rationale: Agents and reviewers systematically skip boundary and security scenarios unless coverage is explicitly mandated.

antipattern.description: AC that reuses the same "Then" clause for multiple scenarios: "Then the user sees a message".
antipattern.reason: Non-unique outcomes cannot be individually verified. Each AC must have a distinct, observable result.

rule.statement: Every NFR must specify a measurement method, not just a target. "Response time SHALL be < 500ms p95, measured server-side under 1000 concurrent users" is complete; "< 500ms" alone is not.
rule.rationale: A target without a measurement method is unverifiable — performance tests cannot be designed without knowing the conditions.

section.name: phase-by-phase-checklist
section.p: A complete spec requires ten phases from brief to approval:

rule.statement: Before writing any requirement, create a Business Value Statement: [WHO] currently cannot [WHAT] because [WHY]. This results in [IMPACT]. By implementing [SOLUTION], we will [BENEFIT].
rule.rationale: Agents and reviewers need the "why" anchor before requirements; without it, Achievable and Relevant criteria cannot be validated.

list.item: Specific: identify exactly which persona has which problem
Measurable: define the success metric (not "feels good" — a number)
Achievable: confirm technical feasibility before writing the requirement
Relevant: link to an actual business goal, not a nice-to-have
Time-bound: assign a MoSCoW priority before moving to user stories
Create minimum 2 distinct personas (name, role, goal, pain points, context)
Personas must differ — not duplicates with different names
Include when/where they use the product (device, environment)
Personas must be specific enough to make design decisions from
Identify high-level user activities (e.g., "Manage Account")
Create backbone: main actions under each activity
Map walking skeleton: MVP stories under each backbone item
Map phase-2 stories separately from MVP — show swimlane boundaries
Verify MVP is independently launchable without phase-2 stories
Format: "As a [persona], I want [action], so that [benefit]" — never omit the benefit clause
Independent: can be delivered without requiring another story in the same sprint
Negotiable: implementation details can be discussed, goal is fixed
Valuable: delivers measurable user or business value on its own
Estimable: team can size it — if not, split further
Small: fits one iteration; if not, split
Testable: at least one AC can be written for it

rule.statement: Write use cases only for multi-step workflows with multiple actors or complex branching. Single-actor, linear flows do not need a use case — a user story with AC is sufficient.
rule.rationale: Use case overhead on simple features adds documentation burden without improving clarity.

list.item: Primary actor, preconditions, main flow (numbered), alternative flows (3a, 4a format), postconditions
Alternative flows must cover validation errors and edge cases
One requirement per FR-X — no compound requirements ("X and Y")
"System SHALL" imperative; no ambiguous words
Include validation rules if input constraints exist
Trace to US-X in the FR body
Assign MoSCoW priority; Must must not exceed 60% of FR count
Performance: response time with percentile and load conditions
Scalability: concurrent user or throughput ceiling
Security: auth mechanism, encryption standard, compliance (GDPR, etc.)
Availability: uptime SLA with RTO/RPO
Accessibility: WCAG level if user-facing
Each NFR: specific target + measurement method + priority
Given-When-Then with specific values (email "test@example.com", not "valid email")
AC-001: happy path
AC-002: validation error
AC-003: boundary condition
AC-004: security scenario (unauthorized access)
AC-005: performance scenario (if Must NFR applies)
In Scope: explicit list of what IS included
Out of Scope: explicit list with reason and timeline for each item
"Not in this release" without a reason is not sufficient — add "Phase 2", "Never", or "v2.0"
Document assumptions about user behavior, system state, external services
Technical constraints: file size limits, API rate limits, browser support
Business constraints: budget, timeline, integration dependencies
Validate assumptions with stakeholders before signing off spec

example.title: Well-formed persona
example.note: Persona: Freelance Developer "Alex". Role: Solo developer building SaaS products. Goal: Ship MVPs in weeks without managing auth/payments/infra. Pain Points: Too much time on boilerplate. Context: Works from home on macOS, deploys to Vercel, uses GitHub daily.

antipattern.description: Persona: "Power User" — goal: "use the product effectively".
antipattern.reason: No role, no pain points, no context. Cannot guide any design decision.

example.title: Well-formed user story
example.note: US-001: As a freelance developer (Alex), I want to create an account with my email, so that I can save API keys and project settings between sessions. Priority: Must (MVP). INVEST: Independent — no other story required; Testable — AC-001 exists.

antipattern.description: As a user, I want to log in, so that I can use the site.
antipattern.reason: No persona specificity, no measurable benefit clause. Cannot validate INVEST criteria.

example.title: Well-formed FR
example.note: FR-001: System SHALL allow users to register with email and password. Email SHALL match RFC 5321 format. Password SHALL be minimum 8 characters with at least 1 uppercase letter and 1 digit. Traces to: US-001. AC: AC-001, AC-002. Priority: Must.

antipattern.description: FR-001: System should validate user input properly.
antipattern.reason: "Should" is not mandatory, "properly" is ambiguous, no validation rules, no trace, no AC reference.

example.title: Well-formed NFR
example.note: NFR-001: Login response time SHALL be less than 500ms at p95, measured server-side (excluding network latency) under a load of 1000 concurrent users. Validation: load test with k6. Priority: Must.

antipattern.description: NFR-001: System should be fast and responsive.
antipattern.reason: No quantified target, no measurement conditions, not testable.

example.title: Happy path AC
example.note: AC-001 (Happy Path): Given user is on /register, And email "test@example.com" is not in the system, When user enters email "test@example.com" and password "SecurePass1!", And clicks Register, Then account is created in the database, And user receives a verification email within 30 seconds, And user is redirected to /dashboard.

example.title: Error handling AC
example.note: AC-002 (Error): Given user is on /register, When user submits password "weak", Then a validation error "Password must be at least 8 characters with 1 uppercase and 1 digit" is displayed inline, And no account is created.

antipattern.description: AC-001: When user submits valid data, then the system responds correctly.
antipattern.reason: "Valid data" and "responds correctly" are placeholders. Cannot be executed as an automated test.

example.title: Well-formed out-of-scope
example.note: Out of Scope: Social login (Google, GitHub) — Phase 2, post-MVP. Reason: OAuth provider complexity. Two-factor authentication — Phase 3. Reason: Added complexity vs. MVP value. SSO/SAML — Not planned. Reason: Enterprise-only requirement, not current market.

antipattern.reason: Does not name specific features. Scope creep prevention requires explicit item enumeration with reasoning.

antipattern.description: Writing spec.md after design.md is already complete.
antipattern.reason: Back-filled requirements are never used for design decisions; traceability is fictional, not functional.
Feature scope is too broad. Split into sub-features; large specs fragment agent context and human review.
Violates 60% Must cap. Forces MVP scope inflation. Re-classify ruthlessly.

section.name: agent-integration-workflow
section.p: A planning subagent reads a product brief or feature request, extracts personas, writes user stories with INVEST validation, produces FR-X requirements with MoSCoW classification, and generates AC-X acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then format. The output spec.md is reviewed by a human and approved before the design phase starts. The faion-sdd-executor-agent later uses FR-X → AC-X traceability to validate that each implementation task satisfies its requirement.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — reads spec.md during task execution to verify AC-X criteria are met
Planning subagent (claude-opus-4-7) — full spec authoring from brief; needs multi-step reasoning for persona+story+AC chain

example.title: Spec authoring prompt
example.code: Given the following feature brief: {brief} 1. Define 2 user personas (name, role, goal, pain points). 2. Write user stories in "As a [persona], I want [action], so that [benefit]" format. Validate each against INVEST criteria — flag any that fail. 3. Write FR-X functional requirements using "System SHALL..." format. MoSCoW: Must ≤ 60% of scope. Each FR traces to a user story. 4. Write NFR-X for performance, security, availability. 5. Write AC-X acceptance criteria in Given-When-Then for each Must FR. Cover: happy path, error handling, boundary conditions. 6. Define In Scope and Out of Scope sections explicitly. Output: spec.md following the template in templates.md.

example.title: Traceability review prompt
example.code: Review spec.md. For each FR-X check: - Does it trace to a user story? - Is the requirement testable (pass/fail possible)? - Is there a corresponding AC-X? Output a traceability matrix: FR-X | US-X | AC-X | Testable | Missing.

list.item: cucumber — Execute Given-When-Then acceptance criteria as automated tests. Install: gem install cucumber. Docs: https://cucumber.io
behave — Python BDD framework for AC-X execution. Install: pip install behave. Docs: https://behave.readthedocs.io
reqif-tool — Import/export requirements in ReqIF standard format. Docs: https://github.com/strictdoc-project/strictdoc
strictdoc — Requirements management + traceability in Markdown/RST. Install: pip install strictdoc. Docs: https://strictdoc.readthedocs.io
Linear (SaaS, Agent-friendly) — FR-X as Issues with custom fields; AC-X as checklists. REST + GraphQL API.
GitHub Issues (SaaS, Agent-friendly) — Lightweight; FR-X as issues with acceptance-criteria label. GraphQL API.
Notion (SaaS, Agent-friendly) — Good for rich spec docs with linked databases for traceability. REST API.
Confluence (SaaS, Partial) — Common in enterprise; agents can write via API. REST API.
Coda (SaaS, Agent-friendly) — Table-based traceability matrices work well. REST API.
StrictDoc (OSS, Agent-friendly) — Markdown-native requirements with auto-generated traceability. File-based.
Write the Business Value Statement before any requirements — agents and reviewers need the "why" anchor
Use FR-X IDs from the first line; never use section headings as requirement identifiers
Every FR must have at least one AC; agent output without AC is incomplete spec
Out of Scope must list specific features with reasons — "not in this release" without a reason is not sufficient
Keep spec.md under 500 lines; if larger, the feature scope is too broad — split into sub-features
Run through the coverage checklist (happy path, error handling, boundaries, security, performance) for every Must FR
Never start design.md until spec.md has human sign-off — design locked to unsigned spec wastes effort
Agents omit "Relevant" and "Time-bound" from SMART criteria unless both are explicitly in the prompt
Given-When-Then output often reuses the same "Then" clause for multiple scenarios — require unique, specific outcomes
Agents will write acceptance criteria that are not actually testable ("system should feel fast") — require quantitative values for all NFRs
Security scenarios (injection, auth bypass) are systematically skipped unless the coverage checklist is in the prompt
Spec-to-design handoff is a human checkpoint; agents must not begin design.md until spec is approved
Large specs (>300 lines) fragment agent context — pass only relevant FR-X sections to design subagent
